serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas is approximately 200 g, comprising two to three pieces of chicken with sauce, served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Chicken with Soy Sauce (Braised); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central Chicken Thigh Cooked and IMR Malaysia MyFCD Ayam Masak Kicap reference values
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exactly matches Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas (spicy variant). Values are adapted from HPB Foodo braised soy chicken entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken thigh (FDC ID 171477) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD Malay chicken dish entries. Sodium reflects soy sauce contribution. Saturated fat estimated from USDA cooked chicken thigh data. Input AI-generated values were reviewed against these approved sources and retained where consistent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Soy-Sauce Based Preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy-braised meat dishes and sweet soy sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with soy-braised chicken and sweet soy sauce dishes in the database. The moderate protein and fat content significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 13.0) / 100 ≈ 5.9 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from sweet soy sauce (kicap manis) and dark soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 200 g serving contributes approximately 700 mg sodium, representing nearly 30% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 45) and moderate carbohydrate content attenuated by high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas has a low estimated GI of 45 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.9 per 200 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may include this dish in a balanced meal but should monitor the sugar content from kicap manis and pair with a controlled portion of rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 2.8 g saturated fat and 350 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken, limit sauce quantity, and keep serving size to 150 g to manage saturated fat and sodium intake within heart-healthy targets.

gout_high_uric_acid.key_factor: Chicken meat has moderate purine content, lower than organ meats
gout_high_uric_acid.guidance: Unlike chicken liver preparations, Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas uses chicken pieces with moderate purine levels; individuals with gout may consume in moderation but should avoid adding organ meats and monitor overall purine intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas is a popular Malay braised chicken dish cooked in a rich, dark, and glossy sweet-spicy soy sauce gravy. Chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs or drumsticks — are first briefly pan-fried to develop colour and texture, then simmered in a sauce built from sautéed shallots, garlic, and ginger, flavoured with kicap manis (sweet soy sauce), dark soy sauce, and fresh red and green chillies. The result is a deeply savoury, mildly sweet, and moderately spicy dish with tender, sauce-coated chicken. It is a staple of Malay home cooking and hawker stalls across Malaysia and Singapore, commonly served with steamed white rice. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 390 kcal, 37 g protein, 20 g fat, and 13 g carbohydrate. The dish is a good source of niacin, vitamin B6, phosphorus, and zinc from the chicken. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 45 (low) makes it suitable for blood sugar management when paired with a controlled portion of rice. The dish is naturally halal and dairy-free. It should not be confused with Hati Ayam Masak Kicap, which uses chicken liver and has a markedly different micronutrient profile including very high vitamin A and iron.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exactly matches Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas (spicy chicken variant). Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo braised soy chicken entries, USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken thigh data, and IMR Malaysia MyFCD Malay chicken dish references. The input AI-generated values were reviewed against these approved sources and retained where consistent; micronutrient values from the input (vitamin A, folate, B12, selenium) were revised downward to reflect chicken meat rather than chicken liver, as the dish name and description clearly indicate chicken pieces, not organ meat. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: IMPORTANT IDENTITY CORRECTION: The input JSON record is titled 'Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas' (spicy soy sauce chicken) but the description text describes 'Hati Ayam Masak Kicap' (chicken liver in soy sauce). These are two distinct dishes. This output record has been prepared for Ayam Masak Kicap Pedas (chicken pieces, not liver) as indicated by the cr_id, name field, and food_group. The micronutrient values from the input that reflected chicken liver (vitamin_a: 3500 mcg, folate: 550 mcg, vitamin_b12: 18 mcg, riboflavin_b2: 1.8 mg, iron: 11.5 mg, selenium: 55 mcg) have been revised to values appropriate for cooked chicken meat per USDA FoodData Central and HPB Foodo data. A separate record should be created for Hati Ayam Masak Kicap if required. Saturated fat (2.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.1 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.1 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), cholesterol_mg (85.0 mg), and water_g (62.0 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken thigh data (FDC ID 171477) as these were not present in the input. Trans fat is 0.0 g (confirmed negligible for home-cooked chicken dishes per USDA data). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this dish. The input listed two default portions (150 g and 250 g); a single 200 g serving has been adopted as the standard serving size consistent with HPB Singapore portion guidance for Malay chicken dishes. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g set to -1 in the input were replaced with estimated values from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken thigh data, as detailed fatty acid and moisture data for this composite dish is not available in primary approved sources.
